stories.headline: Denver police arrest suspect in road rage shooting that killed 26-year-old man
stories.summary:
Denver police have arrested Rayzjuan Curry, 28, in connection with the April 5 fatal shooting of Torin Fluker, 26, near South Broadway and West Maple Avenue. The arrest was carried out with the help of the department's Special Operations Team and Fugitive Unit. Curry is being held for investigation of first-degree murder.

The investigation, which included witness interviews and surveillance video, revealed that Curry and Fluker knew each other and got into an argument before the shooting. Officers initially responded to the area in the overnight hours of April 5 after reports of a shooting, finding Fluker with apparent gunshot wounds; he was pronounced dead at a hospital.

The Denver District Attorney's Office will make the final determination on charges. The case had been described by investigators as possibly stemming from a road rage incident, though police said the two men had a prior acquaintance.

stories.headline: Former Colorado funeral home owner sentenced to 30 years for storing 191 bodies in unrefrigerated building
stories.summary:
Carie Hallford, the former co-owner of Return To Nature Funeral Home in Penrose, was sentenced Friday to 30 years in prison after pleading guilty to 191 felony counts of abuse of a corpse. The sentence came after authorities discovered in 2023 that she and her then-husband, Jon Hallford, had stored 191 bodies in a room-temperature, bug-infested building while providing families with concrete instead of cremated remains.

Jon Hallford had previously been sentenced to a combined 40 years in state and federal prison for his role in the scheme and for defrauding the federal government of nearly $900,000 in COVID-19 aid. Carie Hallford told the judge at her sentencing that her marriage was 'a convoluted web of lies, deceit and abuse' and expressed remorse, though victims' families described lasting trauma.

The Hallfords were arrested in Oklahoma in 2023 after authorities were alerted by a foul odor at the Fremont County facility. In two cases, the wrong body had been buried, and many families who believed they had spread or kept their loved ones' ashes learned they had been given concrete or other debris. The state sentences will run concurrently with existing federal terms.

stories.headline: Pedestrian killed after being struck by trash truck on I-76 in Commerce City
stories.summary:
A pedestrian was struck and killed by a Peterbilt trash truck on westbound Interstate 76 near Commerce City early Friday morning, according to Colorado State Patrol. The crash occurred at approximately 6:09 a.m. at milepost 6, just east of the interchange with Interstate 25.

The truck driver remained at the scene following the collision, and westbound lanes of I-76 were closed for the crash investigation with no estimated reopening time given as of mid-morning. No information about the identity of the pedestrian or where on the roadway the person was walking was immediately available.

Trooper Gabriel Moltrer confirmed the details of the incident. The closure caused significant disruption to morning commuter traffic in the Commerce City area during rush hour.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers introduce bipartisan bill to bolster legislative security after rise in political violence
stories.summary:
A bipartisan group of Colorado legislators introduced House Bill 1422 this week, a sweeping measure that would create a new Administrator of Legislative Safety position to coordinate security for members and staff of the General Assembly. The bill also expands Colorado State Patrol protections at the Capitol, establishes a courthouse security task force, and allows elected officials, judicial employees, and their staffs to request removal of personal information from public online records.

The measure is sponsored by Rep. Chad Clifford, a Centennial Democrat who also runs a home security company; Senate President James Coleman, a Denver Democrat; and Sen. Lisa Frizell, a Castle Rock Republican. The bill was introduced without a fiscal note, leaving its cost to the state unknown.

Legislators cited the June 2025 murder of Minnesota House Speaker Melissa Hortman and her husband as a key catalyst for the proposal. Colorado House Speaker Julie McCluskie noted that Hortman's name appeared on a list found in the Minnesota shooter's car, alongside dozens of other public officials. The bill also responds to a broader national pattern of political violence, including an arson attack on the Pennsylvania governor's mansion and other incidents.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis unveils roadmap targeting $800 reduction in Colorado homeowner insurance premiums
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis released a report Thursday called 'Colorado's Roadmap to Reduce Homeowner's Insurance Premiums,' outlining a set of legislative and regulatory measures aimed at cutting average annual premiums by $800 by the end of next year. Polis and Insurance Commissioner Mike Conway noted that Colorado's insurance premiums have doubled over the past six years, and the state currently ranks sixth most expensive in the nation.

Key components of the plan include a wildfire building code requiring fire-hardened construction for new homes and legislation to fund hail-fortified roofs through a $30 million grant program financed by a 0.5% fee on insurers. A separate bill moving through the legislature would allow homeowners to deposit up to $50,000 annually into tax-exempt savings accounts for mitigation expenses, hail-resistant roofing, or insurance deductibles.

Colorado ranks second in the nation for hail claims, which Conway said account for roughly two-thirds of premium costs statewide. Starting in July, insurers will be required to provide homeowners with wildfire risk scores that account for mitigation efforts, a move officials say is intended to reward homeowners who take steps to reduce their risk. If the measures succeed, Polis projected Colorado would fall from sixth to 13th in the national premium rankings.

stories.headline: Justice Department sides with Elon Musk's xAI in lawsuit against Colorado artificial intelligence law
stories.summary:
Federal attorneys from the Justice Department have filed in support of Elon Musk's xAI in its lawsuit challenging a Colorado law regulating artificial intelligence, arguing the state regulation violates the company's free speech rights. The move puts the federal government in opposition to Colorado's AI oversight statute.

The Colorado law at issue is one of the first in the nation to impose regulatory requirements on AI developers and deployers, and has been a flashpoint in the broader national debate over how to govern artificial intelligence systems. The DOJ's intervention adds significant weight to the challenge and could complicate the state's legal defense of the measure.

The case has implications beyond Colorado, as other states watch whether such AI regulations can survive First Amendment scrutiny. The Denver Business Journal reported the development Thursday.

stories.headline: Avalanche take 3-0 series lead over Kings with 4-2 victory in Game 3
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Los Angeles Kings 4-2 in Game 3 of their first-round NHL Stanley Cup Playoff series Thursday night at Crypto.com Arena in Los Angeles. The win gives Colorado a commanding 3-0 lead in the best-of-seven series, putting the Avalanche one victory away from a first-round sweep.

Game 4 is scheduled for Sunday afternoon in Los Angeles. A sweep would advance Colorado to the second round and provide valuable rest before their next opponent is determined.

The Avalanche entered the postseason as one of three teams on the cusp of completing first-round sweeps as of this weekend, according to reporting from the Denver Post.

stories.headline: Broncos select Texas A&M defensive tackle Tyler Onyedim in third round of NFL Draft
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos used the 66th overall pick in the third round of the NFL Draft Friday to select Texas A&M defensive tackle Tyler Onyedim, trading back to make the selection. The pick comes as the Broncos look to find a replacement for pass rusher John Franklin-Myers, who departed in free agency to the Tennessee Titans after Denver committed more than $400 million in contract extensions to other players over the past 18 months.

Onyedim, who is 6-foot-3 and 292 pounds, is described as a versatile lineman capable of playing multiple spots along the defensive front. He previously played at Iowa State alongside current Bronco Eyioma Uwazurike before transferring to Texas A&M. Denver had no first- or second-round picks in this draft, the first time that has happened since 1995.

The Broncos entered Day 3 of the draft Saturday with additional picks remaining. General manager George Payton and coach Sean Payton have emphasized their confidence in an existing roster that went 15-4 last season before falling in the playoffs.

stories.headline: Nuggets fall to Timberwolves in Game 4, face 3-1 series deficit
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets dropped Game 4 to the Minnesota Timberwolves, falling into a 3-1 series hole in their first-round NBA playoff matchup. The loss puts Denver in a precarious position, needing to win three consecutive games to advance, including two on the road in Minneapolis.

Nikola Jokic's performance has been under scrutiny as the series has shifted in Minnesota's favor. The Timberwolves had previously erased a 19-point deficit to win Game 3 and even the series, before taking the lead in Game 4.

Aaron Gordon has been sidelined with a calf injury, depriving Denver of a key contributor. The Nuggets will need to regroup quickly to extend their season in what has become a must-win situation.

stories.headline: Denver's downtown office vacancy hits 38.9% as remote work culture persists above national average
stories.summary:
Denver's downtown office vacancy rate reached 38.9% in the first quarter of 2026, up from 35.7% at the end of 2024, as the metro area's unusually high rate of remote work continues to weigh on the central business district's recovery, according to data from CBRE. Denver-area workers work from home at a rate of 22.6%, more than double the national average of 13.3%, and Boulder's rate is even higher at 28.7%.

Downtown foot traffic has plateaued near 90% of pre-pandemic levels for the past three years, according to the Downtown Denver Partnership, which hopes the completed renovation of 16th Street will help push activity higher in 2026. Out-of-market visits to downtown, which crashed to 26.3 million in 2020 from 60.6 million in 2019, have rebounded to about 53 million annually but have remained flat since 2023.

Some Denver employers are experimenting with incentive-based programs to draw workers back. Guild, a Denver-based education benefits company, launched a voluntary 'Denver Density' initiative offering a $125 monthly stipend and free lunches to employees who commit to working downtown three days a week, while imposing no penalties on those who opt out. Real estate experts say the outdoor-oriented lifestyle culture that attracts workers to Denver may also be contributing to slower-than-average office reoccupancy compared to other major metros.

stories.headline: Denver software company Render Networks plans local hiring push after acquisition deal
stories.summary:
Render Networks, a software company focused on broadband infrastructure deployment, has struck an acquisition deal and is planning a significant hiring expansion in Denver, according to the Denver Business Journal. The company's CEO said the goal is to make Denver an 'epicenter' for the firm's operations following the transaction.

Details of the acquisition target and financial terms were not fully disclosed in available reporting. The move adds to a pattern of technology firms deepening their presence in the Denver metro amid ongoing growth in the city's tech sector.

The announcement comes as Denver continues to attract software and infrastructure-focused companies, with several firms recently opening or expanding offices near Union Station and in other parts of the city.

stories.headline: Mile High Asian Food Week returns for fourth year with its largest lineup yet
stories.summary:
Mile High Asian Food Week launched its fourth annual event in Denver this week, organizers say the 2026 edition is the biggest in the event's history. The celebration highlights Asian-owned restaurants and food businesses across the metro area through a series of pop-ups, collaborations, and special menus.

Among the featured events is a takeover at MAKfam, a Chinese restaurant in Denver's Baker neighborhood, by Chaatwala, an Indian street food pop-up, where the two concepts will collaborate on an Indo-Chinese menu. The cross-cultural collaboration is emblematic of the event's broader goal of showcasing the diversity within Denver's Asian food community.

The event has grown each year since its founding and has become a notable fixture on Denver's culinary calendar, drawing attention to Asian-owned businesses that organizers say are often underrepresented in mainstream food coverage.

joke.joke: I have had a very long day. I am very small. And I have no money.

topStory.headline: U.S. Envoys Witkoff, Kushner Head to Pakistan for Indirect Iran Nuclear Talks as Tehran Grapples With Strike Damage
topStory.summary:
American envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are traveling to Pakistan for a second round of negotiations aimed at resolving the conflict between the United States and Iran, the White House confirmed. The talks will be indirect, with Islamabad serving as an intermediary to convey Iranian proposals to the American side, rather than hosting direct face-to-face meetings between U.S. and Iranian officials.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi arrived in Islamabad on Friday to consult with Pakistani officials on what the country's foreign ministry described as "ongoing efforts for regional peace and stability." Iranian state television reported that Araghchi had no plans to meet directly with Witkoff or Kushner, and an Iranian spokesman said the foreign minister would subsequently travel to Oman and Russia to continue diplomatic efforts. Tehran's defense ministry struck a defiant tone, stating that "the enemy is looking for a face-saving way to escape the war quagmire it has become trapped in," while White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Friday that "the Iranians want to talk" and "they want to talk in person."

The diplomatic activity comes against a backdrop of significant damage to Iran's infrastructure following American and Israeli strikes. Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian urged citizens to sharply curtail electricity use, telling the public through state television, "Instead of turning on 10 lights at home, turn on two lights," and blaming the U.S. and Israel for destroying the country's energy infrastructure. He also cited a U.S. blockade on Iranian ports as compounding the country's economic strain.

Iran resumed limited commercial air travel Saturday for the first time since hostilities began, with flights departing Tehran's Imam Khomeini International Airport to Istanbul, Muscat, and Medina following a partial reopening of Iranian airspace under a ceasefire. The Strait of Hormuz remains a focal point of tension, with Chevron CEO Mike Wirth saying the U.S. Navy would likely need to escort commercial vessels through the critical waterway once it reopens. The strait has been effectively closed by a combination of Iran's threats and the U.S. port blockade, prompting several maritime incidents in recent days.

In a separate development connected to the broader regional tensions, the State Department announced late Friday that Kuwaiti-American journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin had been released from detention in Kuwait following intervention by the Trump administration. The department offered no further details, citing privacy concerns. The outcome of the Pakistan talks is expected to shape the next phase of diplomacy, with both sides signaling willingness to negotiate while maintaining sharply divergent public postures.
